text: Assessing visual perception is still a very open research area in computer vision. Even though several metrics were designed to measure more or less accurately technical degradations (such as noise, blur, etc. ) these metrics do not translate into natural language and therefore are not related to visual aesthetic feelings. Currently, almost all the metrics estimating the aesthetic or more generally subjective aspects of an image require learning a fully supervised model on datasets where every image has been annotated by experts. In this paper, we show how to get rid of this necessary human expert knowledge and show how to exploit instead, prior knowledge extracted during pre-training a model on vast amounts of image descriptions (contrastive language-image pre-training - CLIP). These priors can indeed be easily re-used on images to estimate not only the technical aspect describing how an image looks (or look quality) but also, more subjectively how an image is felt by its viewers (or feel quality). In other words, we show that some CLIP models which were designed to model only matching between images and text descriptions (and which do not require any specific learning for this task), can measure with high correlations, with or without fine-tuning, the look of an image and its feel aspects. We design also, specific prompt pairs of anti-synonyms describing the evaluation task that CLIP models need to achieve high level of precision. With several state of the art image quality assessment (IQA) metrics on widely used datasets and with designed controlled experiments, our results validate the validity of a CLIP model as a Zero-Shot Image Quality Assessment model, showing that a large amount of useful visual-language priors were already learned in CLIP models and can easily be exploited for instantiating multiple visual perception tasks on images, without requiring any learning at all.
headline: Fully Human Written
